card.title: What Inflation Actually Is
card.teaser: Your grandparents bought coffee for a nickel. Same cup, same bean — so where did that nickel go?
card.coverImagePrompt: A dramatic close-up of a paper dollar bill curling and gently burning at one edge against a black background, warm cinematic lighting, symbolic financial photography

lessons.title: The nickel coffee
lessons.body: In 1920, a cup of coffee in a New York diner cost about five cents. Today that same cup runs close to five dollars. Is coffee a hundred times more useful, more delicious, more energy-giving than it was then? Of course not. The cup didn't change. The yardstick did. That slow shrinking of what a dollar buys is what economists call inflation.

visual.imagePrompt: A 1920s diner countertop with a nickel beside a steaming cup, dramatic lighting, sepia historical aesthetic

lessons.title: Too much money chasing too few goods
lessons.body: Inflation is a general rise in the price level of an economy — not just one item, but a broad basket of them. The classic explanation is Milton Friedman's: 'Inflation is always and everywhere a monetary phenomenon.' If the amount of money in circulation grows faster than the goods and services available to buy, each unit of money is simply worth a little less.

keyTerms.definition: The annual percentage change in an index of consumer prices, usually the CPI
Consumer Price Index — a weighted basket of goods used to measure the cost of living

visual.imagePrompt: A shopping basket with everyday items — bread, milk, eggs — floating upward against price tags labelled CPI

lessons.title: Demand-pull vs. cost-push
lessons.body: Inflation comes from two sides. Demand-pull inflation happens when people want to buy more than the economy can produce — think of stimulus checks colliding with supply-chain shortages in 2021. Cost-push inflation happens when making things gets more expensive — a spike in oil prices, a bad harvest, a labor shortage. Central banks try to read which is which before they react.

keyTerms.definition: Inflation driven by spending growing faster than supply
Inflation driven by rising input costs like wages, fuel, or raw materials

visual.imagePrompt: A tug-of-war rope between two labeled teams — 'demand' and 'costs' — pulling on a giant dollar sign

lessons.title: When inflation goes wild
lessons.body: The Reichsbank printed so many marks during Weimar Germany's 1923 hyperinflation that prices doubled every three days; people carried cash in wheelbarrows and burned banknotes because firewood was more expensive. In 2008, Zimbabwe issued a hundred-trillion-dollar note that couldn't buy a loaf of bread. Both cases share the same root: governments printing money to pay bills they couldn't raise in taxes.
lessons.funFact: A Zimbabwean hundred-trillion-dollar note, worthless in 2008, is now a collector's item that sells for more in US dollars than it was ever officially worth.

visual.imagePrompt: A wheelbarrow overflowing with 1923 German Reichsmark banknotes on a cobblestone street, documentary-style photography

lessons.title: Why central banks obsess over 2%
lessons.body: Most central banks target around 2% annual inflation — low enough that people don't feel their savings evaporating, high enough to give the economy room to grow and to keep the truly dangerous opposite — deflation — at bay. When inflation drifts, they raise or lower interest rates to cool or warm the economy. It's the thermostat of the modern financial world.

quiz.question: Which of the following best defines inflation?
options: A rise in the price of a single important good
A sustained general rise in prices across the economy
Any increase in a country's GDP
The growth rate of the stock market
quiz.explanation: Inflation refers to a broad, sustained rise in the price level — not a single product.

quiz.question: What kind of inflation happens when oil prices suddenly spike?
quiz.explanation: When the cost of inputs like fuel rises, producers pass that on as higher prices — classic cost-push inflation.

quiz.question: Why do most central banks target about 2% inflation?
options: To keep deflation at bay while preserving savings
To match the rate of population growth
Because it matches historical average stock returns
quiz.explanation: A small positive rate keeps deflation risk low while letting savings hold most of their value.

sources.title: A Monetary History of the United States, 1867–1960
